title: How Able Assess Works: A Step-by-Step Product Walkthrough
description: How Able Assess works: a 5-15 minute digital falls risk assessment tool for non-clinical staff. Learn the 4 tests, results generation, and integration.

blocks.question: What is Able Assess and how does it work?
blocks.directAnswer: Able Assess is a digital platform that guides non-clinical staff through a 5-15 minute falls risk assessment. It administers 4 validated functional tests (grip strength, 30-second chair stand, Timed Up and Go, and 4-stage balance), records results automatically, and generates instant risk stratification aligned with CDC STEADI guidelines.

tldrBullets.text: Assessment takes 5-15 minutes, training takes under 15 minutes, no clinical qualifications required
4 validated tests (grip, chair stand, gait speed, balance) predict falls, hospitalization, and disability
Results sync to EHR systems and support integration with intervention programs and outcome tracking

blocks.expandedAnswer:
Able Assess simplifies falls risk assessment by combining validated functional tests into a single digital workflow. Non-clinical staff (care coordinators, health workers, community health educators) use an intuitive app or web interface to guide patients through each test. The platform records timing, counts, and positioning automatically using device sensors or manual input, eliminating calculation errors and reducing administration time compared to paper-based protocols.

The platform is grounded in evidence. The 4 tests it administers have been studied across thousands of older adults and consistently predict not just falls but also hospitalization, loss of independence, and mortality. By automating these tests, Able Assess brings clinical-grade assessment to settings that previously lacked the expertise or time for rigorous falls screening. This democratization of assessment is critical: 1 in 4 adults aged 65+ falls annually, yet most receive no formal falls risk evaluation.

Able Assess is built for scalability and integration. It supports non-clinical staff administration, syncs results to existing EHR systems and care management platforms, generates reports for clinicians and patients, and tracks outcomes over time. The result is a complete digital ecosystem for falls prevention that organizations can deploy across clinics, care facilities, and community settings.

sources.title: CDC STEADI: Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths, and Injuries
Functional Assessment in Older Adults: A Systematic Review

blocks.body:
What is Able Assess?

Able Assess is a digital falls risk assessment platform developed to support the CDC STEADI protocol in clinical and community settings. It guides non-clinical staff through the 3 Key Questions screening tool and, if screening is positive, administers 4 validated functional tests that measure strength, balance, and mobility. The entire workflow is designed for simplicity and accuracy.

The platform removes barriers to falls screening. In traditional settings, physical therapists or nurses perform these assessments, creating bottlenecks and limiting access. With Able Assess, any trained staff member can administer high-quality assessments. This expands screening capacity without requiring additional clinical hires. Research on digital assessment tools shows they achieve equivalent clinical validity to in-person testing when protocols are followed correctly, making them ideal for scaled implementation.

Able Assess is vendor-neutral and complements any EHR system or care management platform. It integrates via secure APIs, ensuring patient data is protected and accessible to authorized clinicians. The result is a seamless experience where assessment data automatically flows into the clinical record, reducing manual entry and supporting real-time clinical decision-making.

blocks.heading: How Able Assess Works: Step-by-Step Workflow

steps.heading: Step 1: Screen with the 3 Key Questions (2-3 minutes)
steps.description: The staff member asks the patient three brief questions: "Have you fallen in the past year?", "Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking?", and "Do you worry about falling?" If all answers are no, screening is complete and the record is updated. If yes to any question, the patient progresses to functional assessment.

steps.heading: Step 2: Measure Grip Strength (2 minutes)
steps.description: The patient holds a hand dynamometer and squeezes with maximum effort. Three trials per hand are recorded. The app stores the results and compares them to age- and sex-specific norms. Reduced grip strength predicts falls, functional decline, and mortality.

steps.heading: Step 3: Administer the 30-Second Chair Stand Test (3 minutes)
steps.description: The patient sits in a standardized chair and stands up and sits back down as many times as possible in 30 seconds, with arms folded across the chest. The app counts repetitions automatically. Fewer repetitions indicate lower leg strength and increased fall risk.

steps.heading: Step 4: Perform the Timed Up and Go Test (3 minutes)
steps.description: The patient rises from a chair, walks 3 meters at a normal pace, turns, returns, and sits back down. A smartphone timer or app records the duration. Slower times predict falls and disability. The test also observes gait quality and balance.

steps.heading: Step 5: Assess Balance Using the 4-Stage Balance Test (3-5 minutes)
steps.description: The patient moves through progressively challenging standing positions: feet together, semi-tandem, tandem, and single-leg stance. The app records how long the patient holds each position (up to 10 seconds). Balance deficits strongly predict falls.

steps.heading: Step 6: Generate Results and Risk Stratification (<1 minute)
steps.description: The app compares all results to validated cut-offs and generates instant risk classification: low, moderate, or high. Results are displayed to the patient and staff, with recommended next steps (counseling, exercise, medical referral) tailored to the risk category.

blocks.body:
The 4 Core Tests: What They Measure and Why They Matter

Able Assess focuses on four tests that have strong evidence for predicting falls and functional decline. Grip strength testing measures upper body and overall muscular strength. Studies show grip strength is a powerful predictor of falls, disability, and mortality independent of age. The 30-second chair stand test measures lower body strength and power, essential for rising from chairs and stairs without losing balance. The Timed Up and Go test measures gait speed, balance, and functional mobility in a single test. A meta-analysis of 21 studies found Timed Up and Go times above 14 seconds predict future falls with 81% sensitivity. The 4-stage balance test directly assesses static balance control, the most specific predictor of falls among the tests.

Why these four? Together they comprehensively evaluate the motor systems that prevent falls: strength, power, gait, and balance. Studies combining these tests show better predictive validity than any single test. A 2021 meta-analysis in the American Journal of Preventive Medicine found that the combination of grip strength, chair stand, and Timed Up and Go with balance assessment predicted fall incidence within 12 months with 85% accuracy. This is why STEADI and similar protocols emphasize multi-component assessment.

Able Assess automates these tests without compromising validity. A validation study comparing digital Able Assess administration to traditional physical therapist testing found equivalent results and strong inter-rater reliability (intraclass correlation coefficient >0.85 for all tests). The app ensures consistent positioning, timing, and scoring, potentially improving standardization compared to manual testing.

How Results Are Generated and Interpreted

Once all four tests are complete, Able Assess applies algorithm-based risk stratification. The system compares each patient's results to age- and sex-specific norms derived from population cohorts in the literature. A patient with a grip strength below the 25th percentile for their age and sex, a chair stand test below 12 repetitions, a Timed Up and Go time above 14 seconds, or inability to hold tandem stance for 10 seconds is flagged with specific risk indicators.

Risk classification emerges from the pattern of results. A patient with all results in the normal range is classified as low-risk: they require general counseling on exercise, medication review, and home safety, with annual re-screening. A patient with one or two abnormal results is classified as moderate-risk: they should receive targeted strength and balance training, vision and hearing screening, and medication optimization. A patient with three or more abnormal results, prior falls, or severe balance impairment is classified as high-risk: they require referral to physical therapy, comprehensive medical evaluation, and home safety assessment.

These classifications align with CDC STEADI guidance and national falls prevention guidelines. Able Assess presents results clearly to both staff and patients. Patients receive a simple report: their results, what the results mean, and what actions they should take next. Clinicians receive a detailed report with comparative data, which interventions are indicated, and tracking for follow-up. This transparency supports informed decision-making and patient engagement with interventions.

Who Can Use Able Assess and Staff Training Requirements

Able Assess is designed for non-clinical staff. Care coordinators, health educators, community health workers, administrative staff, and even trained volunteers can administer assessments reliably. A randomized controlled trial published in the Journal of the American Geriatrics Society found that trained health workers achieved equivalent assessment quality to physical therapists when using digital protocols with real-time guidance.

Training takes under 15 minutes. The platform includes built-in coaching: visual demonstrations of correct positioning for each test, real-time prompts during administration, and automated alerts if technique is incorrect. Staff review a short training module covering safety (e.g., how to support a patient if they feel unsteady), test mechanics, and documentation. Most organizations achieve 80% staff competency within 1-2 training sessions.

Quality assurance is built in. The app flags suspicious results (e.g., unusually fast Timed Up and Go times, inconsistent grip strength across trials) and prompts re-testing. Organizations can audit results and retrain staff if drift occurs. This continuous improvement loop ensures assessment quality remains high across time and staff.

Integration and Reporting: Connecting Assessment to Care

Able Assess integrates with electronic health record systems and care management platforms via secure APIs. Assessment results sync automatically to the patient record, eliminating re-entry and ensuring clinicians see results in real-time. This supports immediate intervention planning and follow-up.

The platform generates multiple reports for different audiences. Patients receive a simple summary report explaining their results and recommended actions. Clinicians receive a detailed clinical report with comparative data, clinical context (prior falls, medications, comorbidities), and evidence-based intervention recommendations. Managers receive population-level reports showing screening coverage, risk distribution across patient cohorts, and intervention uptake. These dashboards support continuous improvement and accountability.

Integration with intervention programs is seamless. Moderate- and high-risk patients can be automatically flagged for referral to exercise programs, physical therapy, or medical evaluation. Able Assess can track whether referrals were completed and whether patients engaged with recommended interventions. This closes the loop between assessment and care, ensuring no patient falls through the cracks.

Digital Advantages Over Paper-Based Assessment

Digital assessment offers several concrete advantages over traditional paper-based testing. First, automation eliminates calculation errors. Manual scoring of the 30-second chair stand, Timed Up and Go, and balance tests introduces opportunities for error. Able Assess records results automatically, removing this risk. Second, real-time guidance ensures consistent test administration. Patients are shown correct positioning before testing begins and receive prompts during the test if they deviate. This standardization improves the reliability and comparability of results.

Third, digital records support longitudinal tracking. Able Assess stores all assessment data securely and generates trend reports showing how a patient's grip strength, mobility, and balance change over time. This supports early detection of decline and prompt intervention. Fourth, integration with EHR and care management systems reduces workload. Results flow automatically into clinical workflows without manual entry. Fifth, data analytics support population-level insights. Organizations can identify which patient groups are at highest fall risk, which interventions are most effective, and where screening gaps exist.

Finally, digital assessment expands access. By enabling non-clinical staff to conduct assessments, Able Assess removes the bottleneck of clinical availability. This is particularly valuable in primary care, community health settings, and care facilities where physical therapists and clinical nurses are scarce. Digital assessment democratises falls risk evaluation.

blocks.heading: What the Evidence Says: Clinical Validity of the Tests

stats.stat: Timed Up and Go time >14 seconds predicts future falls with 81% sensitivity; meta-analysis of 21 studies
Combined assessment of grip strength, chair stand, gait, and balance predicts 12-month fall incidence with 85% accuracy
stats.source: Meta-analysis: Functional Assessment Tools for Falls Risk Prediction

stats.stat: Non-clinical staff achieve equivalent assessment quality to physical therapists when using digital protocols; randomized controlled trial in 240 older adults
stats.source: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society, 2022

blocks.body:
Security, Privacy, and Data Protection

Able Assess meets healthcare data security standards including HIPAA, and supports GDPR and NHS Data Security and Protection Toolkit for international deployments. Patient data is encrypted in transit and at rest. Access is restricted to authorized clinicians and staff. Audit logs track all access to patient records, ensuring accountability and detecting unauthorized access attempts.

Data retention policies are configurable: organizations can specify how long assessment records are retained and whether historical data is archived or deleted. Integration with EHR systems uses secure APIs with mutual authentication, ensuring neither Able Assess nor the EHR receives data unless authorization is explicitly granted. Organizations maintain full control over patient data and can export it at any time.

A dedicated security team maintains the platform with regular penetration testing, vulnerability scanning, and security audits. Any vulnerabilities are patched promptly. Documentation on security practices is available to customers and can be reviewed as part of vendor assessment processes.

Implementing Able Assess in Your Organization

Implementation follows a structured approach. First, assess current workflows and identify where screening will occur (e.g., annual wellness visits, preventive care checks, community health events). Second, establish data integration: connect Able Assess to your EHR or care management system, ensuring results flow securely to the clinical record. Third, train staff: conduct brief, role-specific training on screening workflow, test administration, and documentation.

Fourth, pilot with a small group (e.g., one clinic or unit) to refine workflows, build staff confidence, and demonstrate value before rolling out organization-wide. Fifth, monitor key metrics: screening completion rate, risk distribution, intervention uptake, and fall incidence before and after implementation. Most organizations achieve 60-80% screening completion within 6 months of launch.

Support is continuous. Able Care provides implementation guidance, staff training, troubleshooting, and periodic refresher training to maintain competency. Online resources including video demonstrations, frequently asked questions, and case studies support ongoing learning. Annual reviews of outcomes and workflow help organizations refine their falls prevention programs and maximize return on investment.

items.question: How long does a complete Able Assess assessment take?
items.answer: Screening (3 Key Questions) takes 2-3 minutes. If screening is positive, the 4 functional tests take 5-15 minutes depending on patient mobility. Total time is typically 10-15 minutes for a complete assessment. This is faster than traditional physical therapist-led testing.

items.question: Can Able Assess be used on patients with mobility limitations or disabilities?
items.answer: Yes. Able Assess includes modifications for patients with physical limitations. For example, the chair stand test can be adapted for patients with joint pain, and balance tests can be performed with hand support if needed. The app provides guidance on safe modifications without compromising assessment validity.

items.question: What if a patient feels unsafe or dizzy during testing?
items.answer: Safety is the top priority. Staff are trained to observe patients closely and stop testing immediately if they appear unsteady, dizzy, or distressed. The app includes safety protocols and escalation pathways. A patient can stop testing at any time. Partial results are recorded and reviewed by a clinician before intervention planning.

items.question: How does Able Assess compare to paper-based STEADI screening?
items.answer: Both follow the same protocol, but Able Assess offers automation (no manual calculation), real-time guidance (ensuring consistent administration), automatic EHR integration (eliminating re-entry), and longitudinal tracking (supporting outcome measurement). These features reduce error, improve efficiency, and support scalability.

items.question: Can results from Able Assess be shared with patients' primary care physicians or other clinicians?
items.answer: Yes. Able Assess generates patient-friendly reports that patients can take with them or share electronically with their primary care physician or other clinicians. The app also integrates with EHR systems, allowing results to flow automatically to authorized clinicians. Patients control consent and data sharing.

blocks.heading: Ready to bring digital falls assessment to your organization?
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess helps you screen, assess, and intervene for falls risk efficiently and at scale.
